$comment: Canonical biomarker reference dataset. Committed and HUMAN-REVIEWABLE. Seeded into the canonical_biomarkers table (source='seed') and used as (1) the controlled vocabulary the AI reuses when canonicalizing names, and (2) the reference/optimal bands drawn on biomarker charts. Ranges are general adult values for an unconverted canonical unit. direction: 'higher_better' | 'lower_better' | 'in_range'. ref_* is a lab's standard reference range; optimal_* reflects general longevity literature for adults. Any field may be null (one-sided ranges are fine). Each entry may include 'conversions' (alternate-unit -> factor, where value_in_alt * factor = value in the canonical unit). Regenerate with `npm run gen:biomarkers`. INFORMATIONAL, NOT MEDICAL ADVICE — ranges may be inaccurate and often vary by sex/age.

biomarkers.name: Total Cholesterol
biomarkers.note: Interpret alongside LDL, HDL, and ApoB

biomarkers.name: LDL Cholesterol
biomarkers.note: Lower is better; primary atherogenic target

biomarkers.name: HDL Cholesterol
biomarkers.note: Optimal threshold differs by sex

biomarkers.name: Triglycerides
biomarkers.note: Fasting; sensitive to recent carbohydrate intake

biomarkers.name: ApoB
biomarkers.note: Particle count; strong cardiovascular risk marker

biomarkers.name: Lipoprotein(a)
biomarkers.note: Largely genetic; measure once

biomarkers.name: Glucose
biomarkers.note: Fasting

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin A1c
biomarkers.note: ~3-month average glucose

biomarkers.name: Insulin
biomarkers.note: Fasting

biomarkers.name: HOMA-IR
biomarkers.note: Insulin resistance index

biomarkers.name: hs-CRP
biomarkers.note: Inflammation; avoid testing during acute illness

biomarkers.name: Homocysteine
biomarkers.note: Elevated by low B vitamins

biomarkers.name: GGT
biomarkers.note: Liver/oxidative-stress marker

biomarkers.name: ALT
biomarkers.note: Liver enzyme

biomarkers.name: AST
biomarkers.note: Liver enzyme

ranges_by_age.note: Pubertal growth-spurt peak; falls earlier in girls.

Neonatal physiologic jaundice peaks in the first week then falls; a whole-year band is coarse (it can UNDER-flag a genuinely high bilirubin later in infancy) — interpret against age in days for a neonate. Conjugated/direct bilirubin is NOT raised here (direct hyperbilirubinemia is always pathologic).

biomarkers.name: Carbon Dioxide
biomarkers.note: Bicarbonate/total CO2; mEq/L and mmol/L are equivalent for this monovalent anion

biomarkers.name: Magnesium
biomarkers.note: RBC magnesium is more sensitive

biomarkers.name: Creatinine
biomarkers.note: Varies with muscle mass; interpret with eGFR

ranges_by_age.note: Infant creatinine is low and month-dependent (a birth value reflecting maternal creatinine falls within days); a whole-year band is coarse.

biomarkers.name: eGFR
biomarkers.note: Kidney filtration

biomarkers.name: Cystatin C
biomarkers.note: Muscle-independent kidney marker

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin
biomarkers.note: Reference range differs by sex

ranges_by_age.note: Infant Hb varies widely by month (birth peak, ~2-month nadir); a whole-year band is coarse.

biomarkers.name: Hematocrit
biomarkers.note: Reference range differs by sex

ranges_by_age.note: Infant Hct varies widely by month (birth polycythemia, ~2-month nadir); a whole-year band is coarse.

biomarkers.name: Red Blood Cell Count
biomarkers.note: Reference range differs by sex

ranges_by_age.note: Infant RBC varies widely by month (birth high, ~2-month nadir); a whole-year band is coarse.
Neonatal macrocytosis at birth falls sharply over the first months; a whole-year band is coarse.

biomarkers.name: RDW
biomarkers.note: Higher values associated with mortality risk

biomarkers.name: Ferritin
biomarkers.note: Acute-phase reactant; rises with inflammation

biomarkers.name: TSH
biomarkers.note: Thyroid-stimulating hormone

ranges_by_age.note: Pediatric sufficiency threshold (AAP / Pediatric Endocrine Society) is ≥20 ng/mL, below the adult 30 ng/mL floor.

biomarkers.name: Testosterone, Total
biomarkers.note: Reference and optimal differ greatly by sex; no meaningful combined range

biomarkers.name: Testosterone, Free
biomarkers.note: Highly assay-dependent; differs greatly by sex. Optimal targets removed pending review

biomarkers.name: DHEA-Sulfate
biomarkers.note: Declines with age

biomarkers.name: Cortisol
biomarkers.note: Morning draw; strong diurnal variation

biomarkers.name: IGF-1
biomarkers.note: Age-dependent

biomarkers.name: PSA
biomarkers.note: Prostate marker; age-dependent

biomarkers.name: Oxygen Saturation
biomarkers.note: SpO2

biomarkers.name: Resting Heart Rate
biomarkers.note: Lower in trained individuals

biomarkers.name: Body Fat Percentage
biomarkers.note: Male-oriented optimal; differs by sex

biomarkers.name: Bone Mineral Density T-Score
biomarkers.note: T-score >= -1 is normal; < -2.5 is osteoporosis

biomarkers.name: Visceral Adipose Tissue
biomarkers.note: DEXA VAT mass; units vary by scanner

biomarkers.name: Appendicular Lean Mass Index
biomarkers.note: Sarcopenia marker; differs by sex

biomarkers.name: VO2 Max
biomarkers.note: Cardiorespiratory fitness; strongly age/sex dependent

biomarkers.note: Allergen-specific IgE; <0.10 kU/L = class 0 (not sensitized)

biomarkers.name: PFAS - Branched PFOA Isomers
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - Branched PFOS Isomers
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - Linear PFOA Isomers
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - Linear PFOS Isomers
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - MeFOSAA
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - PFDA
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - PFHxS
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - PFNA
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - PFUnDA
biomarkers.note: Persistent 'forever chemical'; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: PFAS - NASEM Recommended Summation
biomarkers.note: Sum of NASEM-listed PFAS; >20 ng/mL prompts clinical follow-up

biomarkers.name: Aluminum
biomarkers.note: Toxic metal; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: Arsenic
biomarkers.note: Toxic metal; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: Lead
biomarkers.note: Toxic metal; no safe level, ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: Mercury
biomarkers.note: Toxic metal; ideally undetectable

biomarkers.name: Chromium
biomarkers.note: Trace metal flagged on excess

biomarkers.name: Molybdenum
biomarkers.note: Essential trace mineral

biomarkers.name: Amylase
biomarkers.note: Pancreatic/salivary

biomarkers.name: Lipase
biomarkers.note: Pancreatic

biomarkers.name: MCH
biomarkers.note: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin

biomarkers.name: MCHC
biomarkers.note: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration

biomarkers.name: MPV
biomarkers.note: Mean platelet volume

biomarkers.name: Magnesium, RBC
biomarkers.note: RBC (intracellular) magnesium — a distinct test from serum Magnesium, with a different scale

biomarkers.name: Folate, RBC
biomarkers.note: RBC folate (longer-term status) — distinct from serum Folate

biomarkers.name: Methylmalonic Acid (MMA)
biomarkers.note: Elevated MMA suggests functional B12 deficiency

biomarkers.name: Prolactin
biomarkers.note: Sex-dependent

biomarkers.name: Thyroglobulin Antibodies (TgAb)
biomarkers.note: Thyroid autoantibody

biomarkers.name: Thyroid Peroxidase Antibodies (TPOAb)
biomarkers.note: Thyroid autoantibody

biomarkers.name: Rheumatoid Factor (RF)
biomarkers.note: Autoantibody

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin E (Total)
biomarkers.note: Total IgE

biomarkers.name: Omega-3 Total (OmegaCheck)
biomarkers.note: Omega-3 index

biomarkers.name: Arachidonic Acid/EPA Ratio
biomarkers.note: Lower ratio = less inflammatory balance

biomarkers.name: LDL Particle Number
biomarkers.note: LDL-P; atherogenic particle count

biomarkers.name: LDL Small
biomarkers.note: Small dense LDL

biomarkers.name: LDL Peak Size
biomarkers.note: Larger LDL is less atherogenic

biomarkers.name: LDL Pattern
biomarkers.note: Pattern A (large, buoyant) favorable; Pattern B atherogenic

biomarkers.name: HDL Large
biomarkers.note: Large HDL particles

biomarkers.name: Prostate Specific Antigen (PSA), Free %
biomarkers.note: Higher free fraction lowers cancer suspicion

biomarkers.name: Biological Age
biomarkers.note: Estimated biological age; lower than chronological is favorable

biomarkers.name: PhenoAge
biomarkers.note: Levine Phenotypic Age (2018), in years — a derived biological-age estimate from 9 analytes (albumin, creatinine, glucose, hs-CRP, lymphocyte %, MCV, RDW, ALP, WBC) plus chronological age. Interpreted relative to chronological age (lower = biologically younger); a population estimate with several years of error, not day-level precision.

biomarkers.name: Triglyceride/HDL Ratio
biomarkers.note: Derived from the lipid panel (Triglycerides ÷ HDL, both mg/dL). Surrogate for insulin resistance / small-dense LDL; <2 favorable, ≥3.5 suggests insulin resistance. mg/dL-based — a mmol/L ratio differs.

biomarkers.name: Neutrophils, Absolute
biomarkers.note: Absolute neutrophil count (ANC). Adult ~1.5–8.0 ×10^3/uL; varies by age (see age bands).

biomarkers.name: Lymphocytes, Absolute
biomarkers.note: Absolute lymphocyte count (ALC). Adult ~1.0–4.8 ×10^3/uL; much higher in infancy/childhood (see age bands).

biomarkers.name: Monocytes, Relative
biomarkers.note: Monocytes as a fraction of leukocytes (relative count). Companion to the absolute 'Monocytes' (cells/uL) entry.

biomarkers.name: Eosinophils, Relative
biomarkers.note: Eosinophils as a fraction of leukocytes (relative count). Companion to the absolute 'Eosinophils' (cells/uL) entry.

biomarkers.name: Basophils, Relative
biomarkers.note: Basophils as a fraction of leukocytes (relative count). Companion to the absolute 'Basophils' (cells/uL) entry.

biomarkers.name: Nucleated Red Blood Cells
biomarkers.note: Nucleated (immature) red cells as a fraction of leukocytes (NRBC/100 WBC). Normally absent in children/adults; present at birth and clears within the first week (see infancy age band). Companion to the absolute NRBC count.

ranges_by_age.note: Term newborns normally show up to ~5–10 NRBC/100 WBC at birth, clearing within the first week; a whole-year band is coarse (it can UNDER-flag a genuinely raised NRBC later in infancy).

biomarkers.name: Nucleated Red Blood Cells, Absolute
biomarkers.note: Absolute nucleated-red-cell count (×10^3/uL). Normally ≈0 in children/adults; elevated at birth and clears within the first week (see infancy age band). Companion to the NRBC percentage.

ranges_by_age.note: Newborn absolute NRBC is elevated at birth and clears within the first week; a whole-year band is coarse.

biomarkers.name: Immature Granulocytes
biomarkers.note: Immature granulocytes (metamyelocytes/myelocytes/promyelocytes) as a fraction of leukocytes — a left-shift marker. Adult reference ~0.0–0.5% (Sysmex IG%); mildly higher in early infancy (see age band). Companion to the absolute IG count.

biomarkers.name: Immature Granulocytes, Absolute
biomarkers.note: Absolute immature-granulocyte count (×10^3/uL) — a left-shift marker. Adult reference ~0.00–0.03 (Sysmex IG#); mildly higher in early infancy (see age band). Companion to the IG percentage.

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin A
biomarkers.note: Hemoglobin A (α2β2), the dominant adult hemoglobin, as a fraction of total hemoglobin on electrophoresis. The clinically meaningful abnormality is a REDUCED HbA (a structural/variant hemoglobinopathy displacing it), so only a lower bound is set. Physiologically LOW in infancy (fetal-hemoglobin switch).

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin A2
biomarkers.note: Hemoglobin A2 (α2δ2) fraction on electrophoresis. An ELEVATED HbA2 (>~3.3%) is the classic marker of beta-thalassemia trait; a low HbA2 can accompany iron deficiency or alpha/delta-thalassemia. Adult range ~2.0–3.3%.

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin F
biomarkers.note: Fetal hemoglobin (α2γ2) fraction on electrophoresis. Predominant at birth and normally falls to <~2% in adults; an elevated adult HbF occurs in hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin and some thalassemias/hemoglobinopathies. Physiologically HIGH in infancy (fetal-hemoglobin switch).

biomarkers.name: Total T4
biomarkers.note: Total thyroxine. Elevated by high thyroxine-binding globulin (pregnancy, estrogen); Free T4 is more specific.

biomarkers.name: Total T3
biomarkers.note: Total triiodothyronine.

biomarkers.name: Direct Bilirubin
biomarkers.note: Conjugated bilirubin.

biomarkers.name: Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)
biomarkers.note: Assay-dependent; hemolyzed specimens read falsely high.

biomarkers.name: Creatine Kinase (CK)
biomarkers.note: Higher in males; transiently elevated by exercise/muscle injury and higher with greater muscle mass and in some ancestries.

biomarkers.name: Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR)
biomarkers.note: Upper limit rises with age (roughly age/2 for men, (age+10)/2 for women), so this deliberately broad adult cutoff under-flags young adults rather than over-flag older ones.

biomarkers.name: Anion Gap
biomarkers.note: Varies widely by analyzer (direct-ISE methods run lower) and whether K+ is included in the calculation; range is intentionally broad.

biomarkers.name: Reticulocytes
biomarkers.note: Reticulocytes as a fraction of erythrocytes.

biomarkers.name: Reticulocytes, Absolute
biomarkers.note: Absolute reticulocyte count (~25–75 ×10^3/uL; equivalently 25–75 ×10^9/L). Assay-dependent.

biomarkers.name: Estradiol
biomarkers.note: Sex- and cycle-dependent (pg/mL). Female = reproductive-age, spanning follicular→mid-cycle peak→luteal (the app can't know cycle phase), with no low bound so early-follicular / post-menopausal lows never false-flag; the lower post-menopausal range is already covered, so no age band. Male ~10–40. Replaces a prior male-ish single range that false-flagged normal female physiology. When the profile's reproductive_status is set, ranges_by_status overrides this.

premenopausal.note: Reproductive-age envelope (follicular→mid-cycle→luteal); open low bound so early-follicular lows never false-flag.

postmenopausal.note: Postmenopausal E2 ≤~30 pg/mL; open low bound (HRT/atrophy lows aren't flagged). Lets a post-menopausal HIGH estradiol flag.

follicular.note: Follicular→ovulatory envelope (Mayo mid-cycle peak ~498 pg/mL); menstrual dates read this too.

biomarkers.name: Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH)
biomarkers.note: Sex- and life-stage-dependent (mIU/mL). Female reproductive-age spans luteal→mid-cycle (the app can't know cycle phase); post-menopausal FSH is many-fold higher and is resolved via the 51+ age band so it isn't false-flagged 'high'. When the profile's reproductive_status is set, ranges_by_status overrides the age proxy.

ranges_by_age.note: Post-menopausal (age ≥51, median menopause): FSH rises to ~27–133 mIU/mL; the low bound stays broad so HRT-suppressed values aren't false-flagged 'low'. Male ceiling widened for the age-related rise.

postmenopausal.note: Postmenopausal FSH up to ~134.8 mIU/mL (Mayo/ARUP); open low bound so HRT-suppressed values aren't false-flagged 'low'.

follicular.note: Follicular→ovulatory envelope (Mayo mid-cycle peak ~21.5 mIU/mL); menstrual dates read this too.

biomarkers.name: Luteinizing Hormone (LH)
biomarkers.note: Sex-dependent (mIU/mL). Female reproductive-age spans the luteal low → ovulatory LH surge (~95; the app can't know cycle phase) — an envelope that already contains the post-menopausal range (~8–59), so LH needs no age band. Male ~1.5–9.5. When the profile's reproductive_status is set, ranges_by_status refines this.

postmenopausal.note: Postmenopausal LH up to ~58.5 mIU/mL (Mayo/endocrine); open low bound so HRT-suppressed values aren't false-flagged 'low'.

follicular.note: Follicular→ovulatory envelope carrying the LH surge (Mayo mid-cycle peak ~95.6 mIU/mL); menstrual dates read this too.

biomarkers.name: Progesterone
biomarkers.note: Cycle-phase-dependent (ng/mL). Female base is a reproductive envelope spanning follicular (≤~1.5) → mid-luteal peak (~24) since the app can't know cycle phase without a cycle log; open low so follicular/menstrual lows never false-flag. When the cycle log covers the collection date, ranges_by_cycle_phase refines it (follicular ≤1.5 vs luteal ≤24); when reproductive_status is set, ranges_by_status applies. Male ~≤1.4.

postmenopausal.note: Postmenopausal progesterone ≤~0.5 ng/mL (Mayo); open low bound. Lets a post-menopausal HIGH progesterone flag.

follicular.note: Follicular (and menstrual) progesterone ≤~1.5 ng/mL (Mayo). A value well above this on a follicular date is a genuine catch the coarse envelope misses.

luteal.note: Luteal-phase progesterone up to ~23.9 ng/mL (Mayo mid-luteal peak); a normal-luteal value is not flagged 'high'.

biomarkers.name: Grip Strength
biomarkers.note: Maximum handgrip (dynamometer), in kg. A whole-body strength proxy and independent mortality predictor; interpreted by age/sex percentile (Dodds et al. 2014), not a fixed cutoff.

biomarkers.name: 30-Second Chair Stand
biomarkers.note: Sit-to-stand repetitions in 30 seconds — a lower-body strength / frailty measure for older adults. Interpreted by age/sex percentile (Rikli & Jones Senior Fitness Test), not a fixed cutoff.

biomarkers.name: Single-Leg Balance
biomarkers.note: Unipedal stance time, eyes open (seconds; typically capped at 45 s). A postural-control / fall-risk measure; interpreted by age/sex percentile (Springer et al. 2007), not a fixed cutoff.

biomarkers.name: ABO Blood Group
biomarkers.note: ABO group (A, B, AB, or O). An immutable identity attribute — never flagged, never retested.

biomarkers.name: Rh Type
biomarkers.note: Rh(D) factor (positive or negative). An immutable identity attribute — never flagged, never retested.

biomarkers.name: Blood Type
biomarkers.note: Combined ABO group + Rh factor as one result (e.g. "O Positive"), the form many EHRs report. An immutable identity attribute — never flagged, never retested.

biomarkers.name: Protein, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick protein — a screen for kidney involvement. Qualitative (Negative/Trace/1+…); Negative is normal. Distinct from serum Total Protein.

biomarkers.name: Glucose, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick glucose — normally absent; present when blood glucose exceeds the renal threshold. Qualitative (Negative is normal). Distinct from serum Glucose.

biomarkers.name: Ketones, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick ketones — a marker of fat metabolism, raised in fasting, low-carbohydrate states, or uncontrolled diabetes. Qualitative (Negative is normal).

biomarkers.name: Bilirubin, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick bilirubin — normally absent; its presence points to liver or bile-duct disease. Qualitative (Negative is normal). Distinct from serum Total/Direct Bilirubin.

biomarkers.name: Blood, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick (occult) blood — detects hemoglobin/myoglobin. Qualitative (Negative is normal). A microscopic red-cell count is the separate 'Red Blood Cells, Urine' entry.

biomarkers.name: Nitrite, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick nitrite — a positive suggests bacteria that convert nitrate to nitrite (a urinary-tract infection clue). Qualitative (Negative is normal).

biomarkers.name: Leukocyte Esterase, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine dipstick leukocyte esterase — an enzyme from white blood cells, a marker of urinary-tract inflammation/infection. Qualitative (Negative is normal).

biomarkers.name: Urobilinogen, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine urobilinogen — a small amount (up to ~1 mg/dL) is normal; raised levels can reflect liver disease or hemolysis, and absence can reflect bile-duct obstruction.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin G
biomarkers.note: Total serum IgG — the most abundant antibody class, reflecting long-term/adaptive immunity. Low levels can signal immunodeficiency; high levels chronic infection or inflammation.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin A
biomarkers.note: Serum IgA — the antibody class guarding mucosal surfaces (gut, airways). Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin M
biomarkers.note: Serum IgM — the first antibody class produced in a new infection, so a rise can indicate a recent or acute exposure.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 1
biomarkers.note: IgG1 — the largest IgG subclass, directed mainly at protein antigens. Measured in the workup of a suspected antibody deficiency.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 2
biomarkers.note: IgG2 — the IgG subclass directed mainly at polysaccharide antigens (e.g. encapsulated bacteria). A selective deficiency can raise susceptibility to such infections.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 3
biomarkers.note: IgG3 — an IgG subclass active against protein and viral antigens, with the shortest half-life of the four.

biomarkers.name: Immunoglobulin G Subclass 4
biomarkers.note: IgG4 — the least abundant IgG subclass; markedly elevated levels are associated with IgG4-related disease.

biomarkers.name: Leptin
biomarkers.note: Leptin is a hormone made by fat tissue that signals energy stores to the brain; levels track body-fat mass and are strongly sex- and BMI-dependent, so no single reference band applies.

biomarkers.name: Albumin, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine albumin screens for early kidney damage. It is interpreted as the albumin-to-creatinine ratio (normal <30 mg/g), not a fixed concentration, so no band is set here. Distinct from serum Albumin.

biomarkers.name: Creatinine, Urine
biomarkers.note: Urine creatinine reflects urine concentration and is used mainly to normalize other urine analytes (as a ratio); its own level varies with hydration, so no reference band applies. Distinct from serum Creatinine.

biomarkers.name: Alpha-Fetoprotein (AFP)
biomarkers.note: AFP is a protein used as a tumor marker (notably for liver and germ-cell cancers) and, in pregnancy, a screening marker. In non-pregnant adults it is normally low.

biomarkers.name: Carcinoembryonic Antigen (CEA)
biomarkers.note: CEA is a tumor marker used mainly to monitor colorectal and some other cancers. Levels can also be mildly raised by smoking and some benign conditions. The reference cutoff is higher in smokers.

biomarkers.name: Hepatitis B Surface Antigen (HBsAg)
biomarkers.note: HBsAg is a marker of active hepatitis B infection. It is reported qualitatively; a negative (non-reactive) result is normal, and a positive result indicates current infection.

biomarkers.name: Hepatitis B Surface Antibody (HBsAb)
biomarkers.note: HBsAb (anti-HBs) reflects immunity to hepatitis B from vaccination or past infection; a level at or above 10 mIU/mL is generally considered protective.

biomarkers.name: Hepatitis C Antibody (Anti-HCV)
biomarkers.note: Anti-HCV screens for exposure to hepatitis C. It is reported qualitatively; a non-reactive result is normal, and a reactive result prompts confirmatory HCV RNA testing.

biomarkers.name: BUN/Creatinine Ratio
biomarkers.note: The ratio of blood urea nitrogen to creatinine helps distinguish causes of kidney impairment and hydration status; it is a calculated value with no units.

biomarkers.name: LDL/HDL Ratio
biomarkers.note: The ratio of LDL to HDL cholesterol is a summary marker of cardiovascular risk; lower is better. It is a calculated value with no units.

biomarkers.name: Intraocular Pressure
biomarkers.note: Fluid pressure inside the eye (tonometry), eye unspecified. Normal ~10–21 mmHg; above 21 is ocular hypertension, a glaucoma risk factor (not a diagnosis). AAO / Glaucoma Research Foundation.

biomarkers.name: Intraocular Pressure, Right Eye
biomarkers.note: Intraocular pressure of the right eye (OD), by tonometry. Normal ~10–21 mmHg; above 21 is ocular hypertension. Kept as a separate series from the left eye. AAO / Glaucoma Research Foundation.

biomarkers.name: Intraocular Pressure, Left Eye
biomarkers.note: Intraocular pressure of the left eye (OS), by tonometry. Normal ~10–21 mmHg; above 21 is ocular hypertension. Kept as a separate series from the right eye. AAO / Glaucoma Research Foundation.

biomarkers.name: Visual Acuity
biomarkers.note: Clearness of vision, eye unspecified, as a Snellen fraction (e.g. 20/20; 6/6 metric). Qualitative — no numeric reference band, so it is trended as a dated timeline, never flagged.

biomarkers.name: Visual Acuity, Right Eye
biomarkers.note: Visual acuity of the right eye (OD) as a Snellen fraction (e.g. 20/20). Qualitative — no numeric reference band; trended as a dated timeline, never flagged. Kept as a separate series from the left eye.

biomarkers.name: Visual Acuity, Left Eye
biomarkers.note: Visual acuity of the left eye (OS) as a Snellen fraction (e.g. 20/20). Qualitative — no numeric reference band; trended as a dated timeline, never flagged. Kept as a separate series from the right eye.

biomarkers.name: Periodontal Probing Depth
biomarkers.note: Sulcus/pocket depth on periodontal probing, in millimetres (the deepest or a representative site). A healthy sulcus is ~1–3 mm; 4 mm and deeper indicates periodontal pocketing worth monitoring. American Academy of Periodontology.

biomarkers.name: Bleeding on Probing
biomarkers.note: Proportion of periodontal sites that bleed on gentle probing, as a full-mouth percentage. A full-mouth score under ~10% is consistent with periodontal stability; higher suggests active gingival inflammation. American Academy of Periodontology.

biomarkers.name: Clinical Attachment Loss
biomarkers.note: Distance from the cemento-enamel junction to the base of the periodontal pocket, in millimetres — the cumulative attachment lost. 1–2 mm is mild, 3–4 mm moderate, ≥5 mm severe (2017 AAP/EFP staging). Tracks periodontitis progression over time.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Right Ear 250 Hz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the RIGHT ear at 250 Hz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Right Ear 500 Hz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the RIGHT ear at 500 Hz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Right Ear 1 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the RIGHT ear at 1 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Right Ear 2 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the RIGHT ear at 2 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Right Ear 4 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the RIGHT ear at 4 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it — 4 kHz is the classic noise-notch frequency. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Right Ear 8 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the RIGHT ear at 8 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Left Ear 250 Hz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the LEFT ear at 250 Hz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Left Ear 500 Hz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the LEFT ear at 500 Hz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Left Ear 1 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the LEFT ear at 1 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Left Ear 2 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the LEFT ear at 2 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Left Ear 4 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the LEFT ear at 4 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it — 4 kHz is the classic noise-notch frequency. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: Hearing Threshold, Left Ear 8 kHz
biomarkers.note: Pure-tone air-conduction hearing threshold for the LEFT ear at 8 kHz, in decibels hearing level (dB HL). Normal hearing is ≤25 dB HL (WHO); a higher number means the ear needs a louder tone to hear it. Its own per-ear, per-frequency audiogram series. American Speech-Language-Hearing Association / WHO.

biomarkers.name: PHQ-9
biomarkers.note: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 total score (0–27), a validated screening instrument for depression severity. Severity bands: 0–4 minimal, 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe, 20–27 severe. Screening only, NOT a diagnosis. Item 9 asks about thoughts of self-harm. Public domain (Spitzer, Williams, Kroenke / Pfizer).

biomarkers.name: GAD-7
biomarkers.note: Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 total score (0–21), a validated screening instrument for anxiety severity. Severity bands: 0–4 minimal, 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–21 severe. Screening only, NOT a diagnosis. Public domain (Spitzer, Kroenke, Williams, Löwe).
